for the Indian Market

Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
Executive Summary
India's automotive industry is one of the world's fastest-growing economic sectors — ranked third globally
by production volume, contributing approximately 7–8% of GDP and generating around US$250 billion in
annual output (2025). It is a critical export engine, accounting for roughly 8% of the country's total exports.
Yet the sector is facing increasing pressure. The shift to electric vehicles, the rise of software-defined
vehicles, and the complexity of multi-tier global supply chains are creating new challenges. Warranty costs
— currently running at 2–4% of revenue — are climbing. Supply disruptions are an ever-present risk. And
despite vast amounts of data being generated across OEMs, suppliers, and dealer networks, most of it
remains siloed and underused.
Our analytics consultancy addresses these challenges with three integrated, data-driven solutions tailored
specifically for Indian automakers and their partners:
• Warranty Analytics — detect quality issues early, reduce claim costs, and prevent recalls.
• Supply-Chain Risk Prediction — monitor supplier health and logistics in real time, and act before
disruptions occur.
• Dealer & Field Service Intelligence — turn dealer data into actionable insights that boost sales
performance and customer satisfaction.
Together, these solutions have demonstrated measurable returns: leading OEMs using proactive quality
analytics report 5–10% reductions in warranty expense; AI-driven supply-chain planning can cut logistics
costs by up to 15% and reduce excess inventory by 35%; and structured dealer analytics consistently lifts KPI
achievement rates by 10–20%.
This document presents our full offering — covering the Indian market context, detailed service descriptions
with KPIs, data requirements, technical approach, implementation roadmap, pricing models, case studies,
FAQs, and contact information.
US$250B 5–10% 15% ~$20M
India Auto Market (2025) Warranty Cost Reduction Logistics Cost Savings Saved by a Single OEM

Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
Company Overview
We are an independent analytics consultancy specialising in the automotive sector. Our team brings
together automotive engineers, supply-chain specialists, data scientists, and software engineers — all
focused on solving the industry's most pressing data challenges.
We deliver complete, end-to-end solutions: from data integration and machine learning model
development, through to interactive dashboards, automated alerts, and ongoing operational support.
Our approach is practical and client-focused. We work closely with OEMs, Tier-1 suppliers, and dealer
groups to tailor each solution to the client's existing data, systems, and business priorities. Engagements are
flexible — available as fixed-scope projects or ongoing managed analytics services.
Data security and intellectual property ownership are central to how we operate:
• All client data is kept strictly confidential under a signed NDA.
• All models, dashboards, and outputs developed on your data become your property.
• We apply industry-standard security throughout — encryption in transit and at rest, role-based access
controls, and full audit logging.
© 2025 Analytics Consultancy. All rights reserved. Page 5

Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
Market Context — India Automotive
India's automotive sector is a proven growth engine. With production exceeding 2.9 million vehicles in
January 2026 alone, it ranks third globally behind China and the United States. Two-wheelers account for
approximately 77% of total volume, while passenger vehicles represent around 16%. The sector contributes
7–8% of GDP and a comparable share of national exports.
Growth momentum is strong: government incentives such as PLI schemes and the auto mission plan,
combined with major manufacturer investments — including Maruti's Gujarat plant, Volkswagen's Pune
facility, and new EV-related projects — are accelerating expansion. EV sales have been doubling year-on-
year, and over 250 new models are expected to enter the market in the coming years.
Key Market Pressures
Despite this growth, OEMs and suppliers face significant operational challenges:
• Warranty Costs: Global warranty expenses have nearly doubled since 2012, reaching approximately
$58 billion in 2024 (around 2.2% of global auto sales). For some Indian OEMs, this figure exceeds 4% of
revenue — a direct drain on profitability.
• Supply-Chain Complexity: India's logistics costs run approximately 30% higher as a percentage of sales
compared to China, due to infrastructure constraints and inflationary pressures. Multi-tier supplier
networks are difficult to monitor and slow to respond to disruptions.
• Demand Volatility: EV adoption curves, GST policy changes, and post-pandemic recovery patterns all
create forecasting uncertainty that traditional systems struggle to handle.
• Aftermarket Growth: As the vehicle parc expands, the service and parts aftermarket is growing rapidly
— an opportunity that requires better data to capture.
Why Analytics — and Why Now
The answer to these pressures is not more data — it is better use of the data that already exists.
Manufacturers and dealers already collect enormous volumes of information through warranty systems,
telematics, DMS platforms, and supply-chain tools. The gap is in turning that data into timely, actionable
decisions.
Our solutions are designed to close exactly that gap — giving Indian OEMs and their partners predictive
intelligence rather than reactive reporting.
© 2025 Analytics Consultancy. All rights reserved. Page 6

Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
Service Descriptions
1. Warranty Analytics
Warranty claims are one of the most costly and reputationally sensitive areas in automotive operations. A
single undetected defect pattern — left unaddressed for weeks — can escalate into a full recall. Our
Warranty Analytics service is designed to catch these issues early, enabling quality teams to act before
problems reach the field at scale.
What We Do
We consolidate all warranty-related data into a unified analytics environment — including dealer claims,
repair orders, parts usage records, vehicle telematics, and quality test results. Using machine learning, we
detect emerging defect patterns, predict component failure rates, and identify root causes — often weeks
before the issue would surface through conventional reporting.
Key Capabilities
• Time-series anomaly detection on claim volumes and trends
• Clustering of failure symptoms to surface hidden defect patterns
• Correlation analysis linking defects to specific suppliers, plants, or production batches
• Predictive models estimating failure probability within a defined window (e.g., next 6 months)
• Early-warning alerts triggered by statistically significant claim spikes
Business Value
McKinsey research shows that OEMs using AI-driven quality management can reduce warranty expenses by
5–10% or more. Root-cause analysis that typically takes 7+ weeks manually can be compressed to days. One
OEM that linked battery telematics to warranty claim data identified defects weeks earlier than usual,
avoiding approximately $20 million in costs within six months.
KPIs Tracked
KPI Description
Defects per 1,000 Industry-standard quality metric at model and component level
Vehicles
MTBF Mean Time Between Failures — tracks component reliability over time
Warranty Spend per Total warranty cost normalised per vehicle produced or sold
Unit
Claim Cycle Time Average time from claim submission to resolution

text:
Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
2. Supply-Chain Risk Prediction
A single supplier failure or port delay can halt an entire production line within days. In today's multi-tier
supply networks, the challenge is not just visibility — it is anticipating problems before they occur. Our
Supply-Chain Risk Prediction service gives planners the intelligence to act early, re-route, and protect
production continuity.
What We Do
We ingest data from across the supply chain — supplier profiles, inventory and production plans, shipment
tracking feeds, and external signals such as weather, port status, commodity prices, and financial health
indicators. AI models assess risk at every node in the network, simulate the downstream impact of potential
disruptions, and continuously update risk scores as new information arrives.
Key Capabilities
• Graph-based analysis of multi-tier supplier networks to map dependencies and vulnerability points
• Real-time risk scoring for suppliers, components, and logistics routes
• Disruption simulation — modelling the production impact of a supplier failure or material shortage
• Predictive lead-time forecasting for critical parts
• Monitoring of external risk signals: weather events, political developments, port closures, supplier
financial stress
Business Value
Industry analysis shows that AI-driven supply-chain management can cut logistics costs by up to 15% and
reduce excess inventory by approximately 35%. Toyota's application of AI in routing reduced delivery lead
times by 17% and freed significant working capital through a 22% reduction in excess stock. Nissan reported
avoiding seven-figure losses over six months by acting on early risk alerts from a supply-chain control tower.
KPIs Tracked
KPI Description
Supplier Risk Score Composite score combining on-time delivery, financial health, and external signals
Parts Availability Percentage of required parts available on hand at any given time
Days of Inventory Number of days of production that current stock can support
Fill Rate Percentage of orders fulfilled on time and in full
OTIF On-Time-In-Full delivery rate across the supplier network
Production Downtime Hours/days of lost production attributable to supply disruptions

Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
3. Dealer & Field Service Intelligence
Dealer networks are where the customer relationship — and a significant portion of revenue — is won or
lost. Yet most OEMs lack a consistent, real-time view of what is happening across their dealer base. Our
Dealer & Field Service Intelligence service turns fragmented dealer data into a single, coherent picture that
management can act on.
What We Do
We connect to Dealer Management Systems (DMS) and CRM platforms to aggregate data on sales
performance, finance penetration, service bookings, parts turnover, and warranty upsell rates. Analytics
identify patterns — such as which dealers are underperforming on conversions, where service capacity is
mismatched with demand, or which regions have low extended-warranty uptake. For field service teams, we
incorporate visit logs and technician tracking to forecast workload and optimise dispatch.
Key Capabilities
• Consolidated dealer performance dashboard across the entire network
• Identification of underperforming dealers by sales, service, and aftermarket metrics
• Demand forecasting for service appointments by region and vehicle type
• Lead-scoring models estimating conversion likelihood for incoming prospects
• Technician scheduling optimisation based on predicted service demand
Business Value
BI software designed for automotive consolidates data from sales, finance, service, and administration into a
single source of truth — enabling the kind of accountability and targeted coaching that drives real
performance improvement. Networks that have adopted structured dealer analytics have reported 15%
increases in extended-warranty sales within a year and KPI achievement rate improvements of 10–20%.

text:
Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
Technical Approach
We deploy a modern, cloud-based (or hybrid) data and ML architecture designed for reliability, scalability,
and security. The platform is structured around five core layers:
1. Data Ingestion & Lake
Data is collected through secure connectors — database queries, REST APIs, and file transfers — and landed
in a centralised data lake with role-based access controls, encryption at rest and in transit, and VPC-level
network isolation.
2. Data Pipeline & Transformation
ETL/ELT tools (AWS Glue, Azure Data Factory, or equivalent open-source pipelines) clean, standardise, and
structure incoming data. Warranty defect codes are harmonised, supplier networks are mapped, and dealer
identifiers are unified across systems. A metadata catalogue supports data governance and lineage tracking.
3. Analytics & Machine Learning
Data scientists build and maintain ML models using Python-based frameworks (scikit-learn, TensorFlow,
PyTorch) or managed ML platforms (AWS SageMaker, Azure ML). Models include time-series forecasters
(LSTM, ARIMA), anomaly detectors, classifiers, and graph-based network analysis tools. All models are built
to be explainable — producing feature importance scores and root-cause flags, not just predictions.
4. Knowledge Layer (RAG)
A Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) layer is embedded for knowledge management — allowing, for
example, a warranty analytics agent to query technical manuals, recall notices, and repair guides to generate
context-rich explanations. This supports natural-language Q&A interfaces and makes the platform accessible
to non-technical users.
5. Visualisation, Alerts & Security
Model outputs feed into BI dashboards (Power BI, Tableau, AWS QuickSight) with role-based views —
summary KPIs for executives, analytical detail for data teams. Alerts are delivered via email or messaging
platforms (AWS SNS or equivalent). The platform is containerised for flexible scaling and can be deployed
on-premise, in the cloud, or in a hybrid configuration.
Data Flow (Example): Raw dealer data → Secure Data Lake → ETL pipeline (cleanse & aggregate) → Data
Warehouse → ML models (forecast demand, score anomalies) → BI dashboards → Role-based alerts and
reports
© 2025 Analytics Consultancy. All rights reserved. Page 14

text:
Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
Case Studies & ROI Examples
Warranty Analytics — $20M Saved in Six Months
A leading global OEM integrated vehicle telematics data with its warranty claim database using predictive
analytics. By identifying defect patterns weeks earlier than traditional methods allowed, the company was
able to intervene before failures reached the field at scale. The result: approximately $20 million in avoided
costs within six months. McKinsey research corroborates this, noting that proactive quality analytics can
reduce warranty costs by 5–10%. For an Indian manufacturer spending ₹500 crore per year on warranty, a
5% reduction alone equates to ₹25 crore in annual savings.
Supply-Chain Risk — Nissan Avoids Seven-Figure Losses
Nissan deployed a supply-chain control tower with AI-based risk monitoring — tracking weather events, port
status, logistics delays, and supplier financial health in real time. Over a six-month period, the system's early
alerts allowed the company to re-route and pre-empt disruptions, avoiding losses that would otherwise
have run into the millions. Separately, global benchmarks show that AI-driven supply-chain optimisation can
reduce logistics costs by up to 15% and lower inventory by approximately 35%. Toyota's AI-assisted routing
programme cut delivery lead times by 17% and reduced excess stock by 22%.
Dealer Intelligence — 15% Increase in Extended-Warranty Sales
A dealer network that implemented structured analytics across its outlets — tracking warranty penetration,
finance rates, and service KPIs — reported a 15% increase in extended-warranty sales within one year. The
ability to identify and coach underperforming dealers based on objective data (rather than anecdotal
reporting) lifted overall KPI achievement rates by 10–20% across the network.
These examples illustrate a consistent pattern: analytics investments in automotive operations typically pay
back within 12 months. A conservative model — a 5% reduction in warranty costs combined with a 2% uplift
in dealer revenue — is often sufficient for the solution to be self-funding. We can provide a custom ROI
projection once we understand your specific data and cost baseline.
© 2025 Analytics Consultancy. All rights reserved. Page 17

Automotive Analytics Solutions Confidential
Frequently Asked Questions
Q: What data do we need to get started? We work with whatever structured data is available. The
minimum viable starting point includes historical warranty claims (with dates and defect codes), a supplier
and parts catalogue, and dealer sales logs. Richer data — such as telematics, CRM, and procurement records
— produces more sophisticated models. Data cleansing and governance are embedded into Phase 1, so
incomplete or inconsistent data is not a barrier to starting.
Q: Who owns the models and outputs? You do. All models, dashboards, and code developed using your
data are delivered to you and become your intellectual property. In a managed service arrangement, we
retain working copies for maintenance purposes, but full IP ownership remains with the client.
Q: What if we have limited or messy data? This is common, and it does not prevent us from proceeding.
Where key data sources are absent — such as telematics — we rely more heavily on claim histories and
repair logs, and may supplement with industry benchmarks or transfer learning. Phase 1 is specifically
designed to assess data readiness and build a plan around what exists.
Q: How quickly will we see results? Working dashboards and early alerts are typically available within 3–4
months (end of Phase 1). Measurable ROI — cost savings or efficiency gains — generally materialises by the
end of Phase 2 (months 6–9). Timelines depend on the speed of data access and user adoption within the
client organisation.
Q: How is pricing structured? All pricing is custom-quoted following an initial discovery session. Costs are
denominated in INR and vary with scope and data volume. Small proof-of-concepts typically start in the low
lakhs; full enterprise deployments may run to several crores. We can structure payment across phases to
align with delivery milestones.
© 2025 Analytics Consultancy. All rights reserved. Page 19
